nativeRange.habitat: Found in reef-associated environments across a depth range of 2 to 45 metres, typically among live coral, rubble, and sand substrates.

diet.notes: Primarily hunts small live invertebrates such as copepods and amphipods. Has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, and other small meaty prepared foods. Establishing a live microfauna population before introduction is generally advised; training to prepared foods is possible but not guaranteed.

summary: The Griessingeri Goby is a small, cryptic reef fish from the Indo-Pacific, reaching around 3–4 cm. Generally considered reef-safe and peaceful, it is best suited to established nano or reef aquariums with ample rockwork and a live microfauna food supply.
careNotes: The Griessingeri Goby (also sold as Spikefin Goby and Flaming Prawn Goby) is one of only two species in the genus Discordipinna. It is rarely found in the aquarium trade and tends to command a premium price. This species is extremely cryptic and may spend most of its time hidden among rockwork, making it difficult to observe in larger tanks; smaller aquariums with dense rockwork are generally advised for those wishing to view it regularly. A tight-fitting lid is strongly recommended, as these gobies have been known to jump when startled. It is best introduced to an aquarium that already has an established population of copepods and amphipods, as it primarily hunts live microfaunal invertebrates and may refuse prepared foods initially. Unlike many gobies, it does not have a symbiotic relationship with snapping shrimp and does not require a burrow partner. Males are reported to be mildly territorial toward conspecifics in confined spaces; keeping a single specimen or a mated pair is generally advised.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been widely documented. The species has been described as forming pairs, and spawning behavior may occur in well-established aquariums with appropriate conditions, but rearing the larvae is considered extremely difficult given their likely requirement for live zooplankton prey of very small size.

primary.name: Quality Marine
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source used in place of LiveAquaria collections page, which did not contain species-specific data for this species. Quality Marine provided size, temperature, pH, salinity, tank size, diet, and temperament.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes), native range (Indo-Pacific: Red Sea to Marquesas), adult size (3.0 cm SL), and temperature (23–27°C preferred). FishBase temperature range is slightly warmer than retailer recommendations; final values averaged across sources.

additional.name: ReefBuilders
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3–4 cm), tank size (10–20 gal), diet (copepods/amphipods), and behavioral notes. Noted the species is most commonly collected from the Philippines.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (75–77°F = 23.9–25.0°C), salinity (1.024–1.026), pH (8.1–8.3), and tank size (30 gal). The '3 inches' size listed appears to be a unit error for '3 cm'; all other sources confirm the species is approximately 3–4 cm. Lifespan (2–4 years) sourced from here.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: The collections page confirmed the Griessingeri Goby is offered (currently sold out at $148.99) and that gobies in general require a well-sealed lid and rockwork. No species-specific parameters were available at this URL.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been widely documented. Larvae are likely to require very small live zooplankton as prey, which makes rearing them extremely challenging in a home aquarium setting.
